coredata.dc:title: Imaging two targets in live cells based on rational design of lanthanide organic structure appended carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Zhan
Wang, Qianming
Wang, Jinyong
Zhang, Cheng Cheng
coredata.dc:description: New chemical sensors based on lanthanide emission that enable sequential detection of two targets in live cells have been developed. The indicator molecules (unsaturated europium complexes) were covalently immobilized on carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and a water soluble fluorescent nano-probe (CQDs-DTPA-Eu) with biocompatibility was fabricated. A novel stepwise assembly method was used to generate unique responses to two different targets sequentially. Rapid and highly selective detection of tetracycline (Tc) in water and milk (detection limit 32nM) were demonstrated. The in situ generated CQDs-DTPA-EuTc was non-emissive, but, upon addition of hydrogen peroxide (detection limit 21nM) intense red luminescence was observed. Highly reproducible results were obtained in Plat-E cells, demonstrating the identification of tetracycline and H2O2 in cellular environment with high accuracy.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs-NH2)
Preparation of diethylenetriamine pentaacetic acid (DTPA) modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs-DTPA)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Q.M. acknowledges the support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (grants numbers 21371063 and 21328503 ), An Excellent University Young Scholar Fund of Guangdong Province ( Yq2013053 ), and Guangzhou City Scientific Research Fund ( 2014J4100054 ). Z.Z. acknowledges the China Scholarship Council ( CSC ) for financial support. This study was also supported by the Scientific Research Foundation of Graduate School of South China Normal University . The authors sincerely thank for the kind assistances concerning tissue culture and imaging tests by Dr. Yang Geng, Dr. Tongjie Wang, Dr. Yong Dong from Guangzhou Institutes of Biomedicine and Health, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representations of CQDs-DTPA-Eu and the mechanism of luminescence quenching by Tc and enhancement by H2O2. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
TEM images of (A) CQDs-NH2 and (B) CQDs-DTPA-Eu and their corresponding size distributions as determined by dynamic laser light scattering (insets).

Emission spectra of CQDs-DTPA-Eu (100μg/ml) upon addition of 0–100μM of Tc in PBS buffer solution (pH 7.4). Left inset photo: CQDs-DTPA-Eu solution excited by UV light at 365nm (left) without and (right) with Tc (100μM). Right inset: plot of fluorescence intensity (F 0 − F) versus Tc concentration (5–100μM). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Emission spectra of CQDs-DTPA-EuTc upon addition of H2O2 (0–500μM in PBS solution, pH 7.4). Left inset photo: CQDs-DTPA-EuTc solution excited by blue LED light-emitting diodes (λ ex ∼405nm) (left) with and (right) without H2O2 (500μM). Right inset: plot of fluorescence intensity (F 0 −F) versus H2O2 concentration (from 0 to 500μM); the linear range is from 1 to 100μM. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Confocal fluorescence images of Plat-E cells were incubated with CQDs-DTPA-Eu before (A) and after treatment with various concentration of Tc (B, 50μM and C, 100μM) and H2O2 (D, 50μM and E, 100μM). Notes: (I) bright field, (II) blue channel, (III) overlap. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Confocal fluorescence images of Hela cells were incubated with CQDs-DTPA-Eu before (A) and after treatment with various concentration of Tc (B, 50μM and C, 100μM) and H2O2 (D, 50μM and E, 100μM). Notes: (I) bright field, (II) blue channel, (III) overlap. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Confocal fluorescence images of BAEC cells were incubated with CQDs-DTPA-Eu before (A) and after treatment with various concentration of Tc (B, 50μM and C, 100μM) and H2O2 (D, 50μM and E, 100μM). Notes: (I) bright field, (II) blue channel, (III) overlap. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Imaging two targets in live cells based on rational design of lanthanide organic structure appended carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physiology, University of Texas, Southwestern Medical Center, Dallas, TX 75390-9133, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Developmental Biology, University of Texas, Southwestern Medical Center, Dallas, TX 75390-9133, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry & Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China. Fax: +86 20 39310187.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: New chemical sensors based on lanthanide emission that enable sequential detection of two targets in live cells have been developed. The indicator molecules (unsaturated europium complexes) were covalently immobilized on carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and a water soluble fluorescent nano-probe (CQDs-DTPA-Eu) with biocompatibility was fabricated. A novel stepwise assembly method was used to generate unique responses to two different targets sequentially. Rapid and highly selective detection of tetracycline (Tc) in water and milk (detection limit 32nM) were demonstrated. The in situ generated CQDs-DTPA-EuTc was non-emissive, but, upon addition of hydrogen peroxide (detection limit 21nM) intense red luminescence was observed. Highly reproducible results were obtained in Plat-E cells, demonstrating the identification of tetracycline and H2O2 in cellular environment with high accuracy.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemical sensors are used in disciplines such as biology, chemistry, medical diagnosis and environmental science [1–3]. Generally, sensors are equipped with receptors that can transform a chemical process into an analytical signal upon binding to particular guest species. Many advantages make fluorescence attractive [4–6], but high background signals and effects of scattered light have led researchers to search for alternatives. The long-lived emissions of rare earth elements differ significantly from molecular fluorescence derived from organic compounds. In addition, rare earth elements have favorable features including a wide gap between excitation and emission, high color purity, and narrow emission peaks [7–9].

Numerous organic chromophores such as aromatic acids and β-diketones efficiently sensitize lanthanide luminescence through energy transfer; however, the corresponding complexes were hard to protect the lanthanide centers against the external molecules which would try to quench the luminescence. Here we used the multi-dentate type ligand as a stable building block in the preparation of complex. Following this idea, we have selected the use of the analyte for the first time as a sensitizer for europium luminescence in the red region. It is well accepted that common organic complexes suffered from their instabilities upon exposure to light or heat [10,11]. Therefore, the next step to the assembly of a stable sensor is the production of organic–inorganic hybrid materials by the incorporation of the indicator into the solid host. The most frequently used method for the immobilization is called physical doped route. However, this direct entrapment of the optical centers in the inorganic network may cause inhomogeneous dispersion within the material and reduce stability in terms of leaching problem. In this study, we used the covalent attachment of the organic structure to the matrice as the alternative approach in order to improve the stability and reproducibility of the sensor.

Host materials such as silica, titania, quantum dots, soft matters, carbon nanotubes, graphene, and others have been integrated with molecular receptors to generate sensitive luminescent hybrids [12–16]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received much attention recently due to their superior optical properties, including tunability and non-blinking and stable luminescent emission [17,18]. To extend the application of CQDs in sensing and imaging field, organic compounds and polymer functionalized CQDs nanohybrids have been designed and used in detection of specific analytes [19–22]. CQDs are water soluble, ultra-small, and biocompatible [23–27]. However, use of smart fluorescent carbon dots that use lanthanide emission to signal guest recognition have not yet been reported.

For many applications, a response to two or more guest species would be very valuable. Here we focused development of lanthanide-based sequential-type sensors of tetracycline (Tc) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Tc is widely used in veterinary medicine [28–30]. H2O2 is used in food processing and in the chemical and environmental industries in large amounts [31,32]. H2O2 is one of the products of the activity of almost all oxidases [33] and transmits signals in organisms related to cell proliferation, migration, and differentiation [34]. Therefore, we designed and prepared novel hybrid nano-probes for recognition of Tc and H2O2 in aqueous solution (Fig. 1 ). In the presence of Tc, the blue emission of CQDs-DTPA-Eu was turned off since Tc takes part in the coordination with europium ion to form the complex (CQDs-DTPA-EuTc), and fluorescence static quenching occurred. Upon binding of hydrogen peroxide to CQDs-DTPA-EuTc significant luminescence enhancement was observed due to the replacement of solvents. Especially in the range of bioscience, studies of cellular processes usually require the visualization of specific targets in biological media. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first probe that enables analysis of changes in levels of two important molecules inside live cells based on carbon dots and europium ions.

Eu2O3 (99.9%) was purchased from Shanghai Yuelong Company. Europium chloride was obtained by dissolving Eu2O3 in concentrated hydrochloric acid. Citric acid (AR), ethylenediamine (99%), diethylenetriamine pentaacetic acid dianhydride (98%), tetracycline hydrochloride (USP), amino acids (Ala, Arg, Asp, Glu, His, Lys, and Cys), reduced glutathione (GSH), ascorbic acid (AA), and glucose were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36–38%), sodium carbonate (99%), sodium bicarbonate (99%), disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (99%), sodium dihydrogen phosphate dehydrate (99%) and all the other reagents were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory and used without further purification. Annexin V-PE/7-AAD kit was purchased from the BD Biosciences (USA). Dulbecco’s modified medium (DMEM), endothelial basal medium (EBM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), and antibiotics penicillin (PS) were purchased from Lonza (USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs-NH2)
ce:section.ce:para: Both CQDs and CQDs-NH2 were synthesized according to a literature procedure with slight modifications [35]. Citric acid (1.0g) was dissolved in 10ml of deionized water in a 50-ml beaker under vigorous stirring. The transparent solution without or with ethylenediamine (EDA, 1.0ml) was microwaved for 8min at 750W. After cooling to room temperature, 5ml deionized water was added to dissolve the formed CQDs (without EDA) or CQDs-NH2 (with EDA). The resultant mixture was then centrifuged, and the supernatant was dialyzed against pure water (membrane MWCO of 1000) for 3days. CQDs or CQDs-NH2 was then precipitated from the dispersion, washed twice with anhydrous ethanol, and dried under vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of diethylenetriamine pentaacetic acid (DTPA) modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs-DTPA)
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs-NH2 (100mg) was dissolved by sonication in 10ml of bicarbonate buffer (0.1M, pH 9.6) followed by addition of N,N-Diisopropylethylamine (DIPEA) as a catalyst. Then, 2ml (0.1M) of diethylenetriamine pentaacetic acid dianhydride (DTPAda) DMSO solution was slowly added and stirred at room temperature for 48h in the darkness. CQDs-DTPA was precipitated, washed three times with anhydrous ethanol, and subjected to dialysis against pure water for 48h to remove the excessive DTPAda, followed by drying in vacuum before use. The amount of DTPA on the surface of CQDs (0.27mmol/g) could be determined through the following measurement of Eu3+ (see following Section 2.4) since it forms 1:1 coordination complex with europium ions.

CQDs-DTPA (50mg) was dissolved in 10ml of Tris–HCl buffer solution (0.05M, pH 7.4). A EuCl3·6H2O aqueous solution (1ml, 0.01M) was added dropwise with stirring at room temperature in the dark. After 24h, the solution was dialyzed against water for 2days to remove the excess europium ions. The amount of the europium complexes on the surface of carbon dots is determined to be 0.27mmol/g by the elemental analysis and complexation titration (Table S1). The final concentration of the probe solution was around 10mg/ml as determined by the dry weight analysis method. The dispersion was then stored at 4°C.

Plat-E cells (human embryonic kidney cells) and Hela cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% antibiotics penicillin (PS) and maintained at 37°C with 5% CO2. Bovine aortic endothelial cells (abbreviated as BAEC) were cultured in endothelial basal medium (EBM, Lonza, USA) containing 10% newborn calf serum (FBS) in humidified atmosphere incubator containing 5% CO2 at 37°C. To investigate apoptosis analysis, untreated cells and cells treated with CQDs-DTPA-Eu were both stained with Annexin V-PE/7-AAD. Plat-E and Hela cells (106 cells/well) were dispersed within replicated 12-well microtiter plates and incubated for 24h at 37°C with 5% CO2. After removal of the medium, cells were incubated with fresh medium (DMEM, 10% FBS and 1% PS) (BAEC cells were incubated with EBM medium) containing CQDs-DTPA-Eu (0, 5, 10, 20, 50, and 100μg/ml), CQDs-DTPA-Eu (100μg/ml) and Tc (5, 10, 20, 50, or 100μM), CQDs-DTPA-Eu (100μg/ml), Tc (100μM) and H2O2 (5, 10, 20, 50, or 100μM) for another 24h. After washing with bonding buffer twice, centrifuging, and adding another 100μl bonding buffer, 3μl of 7-AAD and Annexin V-PE were added to each sample. Samples were incubated for 15min and analyzed by flow cytometry on a BD Accuri C6 Flow Cytometer. In this study, the reasons to choose Hela cells and Plat-E cells that were widely used in biomedical research were due to their stability and safety. Since tetracycline was frequently used as the veterinary medicine, we further investigated the cell-imaging features in livestock’s cell (BAEC cells) in order to find its potential uses.

Three cell lines (Plat-E, HeLa, and BAEC) were used in this study. Both of Hela and Plat-E cells were cultured in DMEM media with 10% FBS and 1% antibiotics penicillin and maintained at 37°C with 5% CO2. The BAEC cells were incubated in EBM media with 10% FBS at 37°C with 5% CO2. The three cell lines within replicate 96-well plates were pre-cultured at standard culture condition for one day. After removal of the medium, cells were incubated with fresh media containing CQDs-DTPA-Eu (0, 5, 10, 20, 50, and 100μg/ml), CQDs-DTPA-Eu (100μg/ml) and Tc (10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 100μM), CQDs-DTPA-Eu (100μg/ml), Tc (100μM) and H2O2 (10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 100μM) for another 24h. Then 20μl MTT solution was added to each well. After incubated with 4h, the MTT solution was removed, followed by adding DMSO (100μl each well) to dissolve the formed crystals. The absorbance was measured at 490nm using a Polarstar microplate reader.

The three cell lines were incubated at standard culture condition (as mentioned above) for one day. For the experiments, cells were washed with media, incubated in the fresh media containing CQDs-DTPA-Eu (100μg/ml) at 37°C under 5% CO2 for 2h and then were treated with 50μM Tc, 100μM Tc, the mixture of 100μM Tc and 50μM H2O2, or the mixture of 100μM Tc and 50μM H2O2 for 30min. Cells were washed three times with PBS and imaged on a Leica confocal laser scanning microscope (TCS SP2 AOBS) equipped with a UV laser (405nm).

The two cell lines (HeLa cells and Plat-E) cultured at standard culture conditions were treated with CQDs-DTPA-Eu (100μg/ml) and immediately maintained at 4°C for 2h. For the ATP-depleted environments, cells were pre-incubated with 2-deoxy-d-glucose (50mM) and sodium azide (10mM) for 30min at 37°C, subsequently cells were washed with PBS three times and followed incubated with CQDs-DTPA-Eu (100μg/ml).

Fluorescence and excitation spectra were measured using a Hitachi-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a 50-W mercury lamp as light source. UV–vis spectra were recorded on an Agilent 8453 spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectra were measured by Prestige-21 (Shimadzu). TEM images and HR-TEM images were obtained with a JEOL JEM-2100HR transmission electron microscope. Lifetimes were detected using an Edinburgh FLS920 spectrometer. Dynamic light scattering measurements were performed on a Malvern Nano-ZS90 particle size analyzer. Confocal microscopy images were taken with a Leica confocal laser scanning microscope (TCS SP2 AOBS) equipped with a laser at 405nm. Flow cytometric analysis was performed on BD Accuri C6 Flow Cytometer. The photostabilities of CQDs-DTPA-Eu and acridine orange were compared by exposure of the two solutions under a 50W LED lamp after 24h.

Diethylenetriamine pentaacetic acid dianhydride (DTPAda) was chosen as the organic ligand to link the CQDs and metal ions for several reasons. First, DTPAda can react with the amino-functionalized CQDs (CQDs-NH2 ) to yield multiple carboxyl groups bearing CQDs (CQDs-DTPA). Secondly, the hydrophilic CQDs-DTPA does not induce aggregation of nanoparticles after coordination with europium ions. The morphologies of CQDs-NH2 and CQDs-DTPA-Eu were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 2 A and B), and the size distributions were measured by dynamic laser light scattering (DLS) (Fig. 2A and B, inset). Both CQDs-NH2 and CQDs-DTPA-Eu exhibited homogenous distributions; uniform spherical structures with average diameters of about 2.1 and 2.9nm, respectively, were observed. These data indicated that loading of europium complexes on the CQDs did not alter shapes or dispersion. In the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs-NH2 , strong absorption bands at 1645, 1564, and 1400cm−1 correspond to CC stretching, NH bending vibration, and amido CN stretching, respectively (Fig. S1). After the attachment of DTPA-Eu complexes, new peaks at around 2909–2997 and 949cm−1 (CH and CN stretching of DTPA, respectively) were observed. In addition, evidence for the coordination DTPA with europium ion was provided by the bands located at 1653 and 1443cm−1 that are indicative of asymmetric and symmetric stretching modes of carboxylate.

The zeta-potentials of Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), CQDs-NH2 and CQDs-DTPA-Eu were recorded at pH 7.0. After surface passivation with ethylenediamine (EDA), the charge density of carbon quantum dots was decreased from −38.5 to −15.7mV, indicating that the amine groups were attached on the surface of carbon quantum dots [36,37]. After conjugation with europium complexes (DTPA-Eu), CQDs-DTPA-Eu still bears negative charge (−21.6mV) that may be promising in the application of biological detection.

The fluorescence behavior of CQDs-DTPA-Eu solution (100μg/ml) in the presence of Tc was investigated by fluorescence titration experiments. CQDs-DTPA-Eu displayed a strong blue emission before reacting with Tc (λ ex =356nm); however, the peak intensities significantly declined and were almost completely quenched upon the addition of Tc (Fig. 3 ). Distinguishable changes could be observed with the naked eye under UV light (Fig. 3, left inset). The relative fluorescence intensity at 456nm (F 0 − F) changed linearly with Tc concentration (Fig. 3, right inset). The detection of limit (DL) was 32nM as calculated according to the equation DL=3×SD/slope, where SD is the standard deviation of the blank sample (obtained by 10 consecutive scans of the blank sample).

Selectivity is critical for fluorescent sensors to be used in biological matrices. At high concentrations (10 equiv. of Tc concentration), interference with the signal due to Tc by amino acids, glucose, vitamin, and peptide were almost negligible (Fig. S2). In order to demonstrate accuracy and reliability, our method was applied to monitor Tc in milk samples (see detailed procedures in ESI). As indicated by the data shown in Table S2, accuracy and RSD were generally acceptable. These results suggest that CQDs-DTPA-Eu is suitable for the detection of Tc in biological samples.

To explore the sensing mechanism, the absorption spectra and emission lifetimes of CQDs-DTPA-Eu in the absence or presence of Tc were measured. CQDs-DTPA-Eu exhibited a single peak at 350nm, but a new absorption band located around 400nm appeared with the increasing amounts of Tc (Fig. S3). Furthermore, the absorption bands (at 270 and 381nm) of pure Tc were not observed in this solution. It suggested that Tc would interact with the ground state of the organic structures on the surface of carbon dots and the photoluminescence intensities were suppressed. More importantly, the lifetime of CQDs-DTPA-Eu was not altered by addition of Tc (Fig. S4). Therefore, the sensing mechanism likely results from coordination between Tc and europium ion to form a complex (CQDs-DTPA-EuTc), and detection is ascribed to a static quenching process.

Detection of hydrogen peroxide has been challenging. To develop a robust and sensitive method of sensing H2O2 that relies on the Eu(III) containing organic–inorganic hybrid network we chose to use CQDs-DTPA-Eu, which was almost quenched at the high concentration of Tc (100μM, Fig. 3). As shown in Fig. 4 , upon excitation at 405nm, bands were observed at 579, 591, 616, 652 and 698nm, which correspond to the characteristic transitions of the Eu3+ from 5D0 energy level to 7F J multiplets (J =0, 1, 2, 3 and 4, respectively). Upon the addition of H2O2 at concentrations from 1 to 500μM, the emission intensity of CQDs-DTPA-EuTc was enhanced, especially at 616nm. The relative intensity of luminescence increased from 24 to 575 within 3–5s of addition of H2O2, and the color change could be observed with the naked eyes (Fig. 4, left inset). The plot of F − F 0 versus concentration of H2O2 (from 1 to 100μM) was linear (Fig. 4, right inset), and the detection limit of H2O2 in solution was 21nM.

The excitation spectrum of CQDs-DTPA-EuTc varied slightly when H2O2 was added (Fig. S5). But the emission intensity of CQDs-DTPA-EuTc was enormously enhanced in the presence of H2O2. It has also been found that no apparent changes were detected in CQDs-DTPA-Eu (Figs. S6 and S7), indicating that only H2O2 would not affect the energy levels of the donors. It has been well documented that the strongly absorbing chromophore (here as Tc) will be necessary to sensitize lanthanide emissions. H2O2 participated into the complex and replaced the coordinated water molecules [33]. No changes in the excitation spectrum were observed upon addition of 1mM of alkali metal, alkali-earth metal, halide ion, ammonium, sulfate, or nitrate anion (Fig. S8). These results indicate that the in situ-generated CQDs-DTPA-EuTc can be used as a specific fluorescent probe for H2O2.

We also demonstrated that CQDs-DTPA-Eu can be used to recognize intracellular tetracycline and hydrogen peroxide. First, the cytotoxicity of CQDs-DTPA-Eu was evaluated based on three cell lines (Hela, Plat-E and BAEC) and the flow cytometric analysis was carried out. Annexin V-PE/7-AAD kit were utilized to evaluate the apoptosis of cells in presence of CQDs-DTPA-Eu, Tc, and H2O2. Fig. S9 showed the 7-AAD with annexin V-PE analysis of the Hela cells. Viable cells are negative for both PE and 7-AAD (Q4 region in Fig. S9), early apoptotic cells are PE positive and 7-AAD negative (Q1 region in Fig. S9), late apoptotic or dead cells are positive both PE and 7-AAD (Q2 region in Fig. S9), while the damaged cells were located in the region of Q3. No obvious differences were observed between cells treated with 100μg/ml of CQDs-DTPA-Eu for 24h and untreated cells (Fig. S9). The similar results were obtained in the Plat-E and BAEC cells (Figs. S10 and S11).

To further clarify the cytotoxicity of CQDs-DTPA-Eu in the presence or absence of Tc or H2O2, methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay was used (Figs. S12–S14). Following incubation with CQDs-DTPA-EuTc (100μM) and H2O2 (100μM) for 24h, the cellular viabilities of Plat-E and HeLa cells were well above 92% and 93%, indicating that the added luminescent species had very low cytotoxicity.

After the cells were incubated with CQDs-DTPA-Eu for 2h, confocal microscopic images showed very strong intracellular blue luminescence was clearly observed in CQDs-DTPA-Eu treated cells (Figs. 5 A and 6 A). These blue emissions were turned off upon exposure of cells to 50 or 100μM of Tc, indicating that tetracycline can be detected inside living cells (Figs. 5B, C and 6B, C). Subsequently, the Tc-treated cells were incubated with H2O2 for 10min at 37°C. Red emissions were almost immediately observed that was dependent on H2O2 concentration (Figs. 5D, E and 6D, E). Because tetracycline was generally used as veterinary medicine, we continue to explore the cell-imaging features in livestock’s cell (bovine aortic endothelial cell). The luminescence responses exhibited similar results (Fig. 7 ). These multicolor imaging tests demonstrate that CQDs-DTPA-Eu can sequentially sense two targets in biological media. To confirm the stability of the CQDs-DTPA-Eu in culture medium, we choose a commercial dye acridine orange (AO), a cell-permeable dye, as the reference substance. In this study, a 50W LED lamp was used to irradiate the culture media containing CQDs-DTPA-Eu and AO, respectively. As shown in the following figures, the emission of AO decreased over 50% in 3h, it was almost completely quenched after 10h of light irradiation. However, there were no significant changes in the emission of CQDs-DTPA-Eu even it was irradiated under the same light source for 24h (Fig. S15).

To investigate the intracellular transport mechanism of CQDs-DTPA-Eu, we cultured the cells at low temperature (4°C) or in ATP-depleted environments because endocytosis as the energy dependent route can be blocked by the above two ways (further details see Section 2) [38]. As shown in Figs. S16 and S17, cells incubated with CQDs-DTPA-Eu at 4°C displayed slight blue emission compared with that performed at 37°C. The cells in ATP-depleted environments also showed similar results, proving that the predominant intracellular transport of CQDs-DTPA-Eu was the endocytosis pathway.

We have developed a new nano-probe based on lanthanide emission and carbon quantum dots, CQDs-DTPA-Eu. In the presence of tetracycline a turn-off signal (from blue to dark) fluorescent quenching effect was observed. A turn-on signal (from dark to red) was observed upon exposure to H2O2. This probe detected Tc in milk and permeated the cell membrane to detect changes in intracellular H2O2 levels. Our study demonstrated non-toxicity, low detection limits and biocompatibility of the nano-probe. This sensor takes a critical step for real world diagnosis by using lanthanide-CQD sensors and we will further improve its differentiability.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Q.M. acknowledges the support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (grants numbers 21371063 and 21328503), An Excellent University Young Scholar Fund of Guangdong Province (Yq2013053), and Guangzhou City Scientific Research Fund (2014J4100054). Z.Z. acknowledges the China Scholarship Council (CSC) for financial support. This study was also supported by the Scientific Research Foundation of Graduate School of South China Normal University. The authors sincerely thank for the kind assistances concerning tissue culture and imaging tests by Dr. Yang Geng, Dr. Tongjie Wang, Dr. Yong Dong from Guangzhou Institutes of Biomedicine and Health, Chinese Academy of Sciences.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.05.107.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular recognition at the gas-solid interface: a powerful tool for chemical sensing
Chemical modulation of work function as a transduction mechanism for chemical sensors
Directed assembly metallocyclic supramolecular systems for molecular recognition and chemical sensing
Chemically assembled monolayers of fluorophores as chemical sensing materials
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Lanthanide luminescence for biomedical analyses and imaging
Cocktails of Tb3+ and Eu3+ complexes: a general platform for the design of ratiometric optical probes
Photostability of a highly luminescent europium β-diketonate complex in imidazolium ionic liquids
